title: The Fourth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Sunday, July 3, 2022 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fourth Sunday after Pentecost, placing the liturgical calendar in Ordinary Time (the long green season running from Pentecost through Advent). The RCL readings for this date would be 2 Kings 5:1-14 (Naaman's healing), Psalm 30, Galatians 6:1-16 (Paul on bearing one another's burdens), and Luke 10:1-11, 16-20 (Jesus sending out seventy disciples). The emphasis falls on themes of healing, community responsibility, and mission work within the Christian life.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the midst of its triennial General Convention, held that year in Baltimore (July 7-14, 2022), just days after this sermon was preached. Major pre-Convention tensions included debates over sexuality and marriage equality, with various resolutions pending that would shape the church's official positions on LGBTQ+ inclusion. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Marty Stebbins (elected 2017), was navigating broader denominational divisions while maintaining its own pastoral commitments.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing a significant heat wave in late June/early July 2022, with temperatures exceeding 100°F and creating drought conditions affecting the region. Locally, there was ongoing coverage of the Gabby Petito murder case aftermath (Petito was from Long Island but the case dominated national and Colorado news throughout summer 2022). The Colorado Springs area had experienced the Ute Pass Fire in late June, adding to wildfire season awareness in the state.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Supreme Court's overturning of Roe v. Wade on June 24, 2022 (nine days prior) dominated national discourse and religious community reactions. The January 6 Committee was conducting televised hearings on the Capitol riot, with new testimony from Trump administration officials and Secret Service personnel being broadcast that week. The Biden administration faced criticism over inflation, gas prices exceeding $5 per gallon nationally, and economic recession fears heading into the midterm election season.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Russia's invasion of Ukraine (ongoing since February 2022) continued with intense fighting in the Donbas region; Kyiv was struck by Russian missiles on July 1-2, killing civilians and destroying infrastructure. NATO had just formalized Swedish and Finnish applications to join the alliance at its summit in Madrid (June 28-30), a major geopolitical shift prompted by Russian aggression. Monkeypox cases were rising globally and in the United States, leading to increased public health messaging.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was anxious and polarized following the Roe decision; religious communities were particularly divided over the ruling, with Episcopal and mainline Protestant voices offering varied theological responses. The July 4th holiday weekend was approaching, creating a moment of reflection on national identity amid deep cultural fractures. The COVID-19 pandemic had transitioned to "endemic" status in most American consciousness by summer 2022, though new variants circulated; most restrictions had been lifted, but collective trauma lingered.
